title: Neolabatia parviflora
taxonomy: Neolabatia parviflora belongs to the family Anacardiaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the cashew family. This family consists of about 83-90 genera and more than 800 species, with many of these species being native to tropical and subtropical regions of the world. Neolabatia parviflora is a species of flowering plant that is endemic to Madagascar. It is a small, deciduous shrub with small, yellow flowers and is often found in dry forests and woodlands. The exact classification of Neolabatia parviflora within the broader plant kingdom is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Rosids.
uses: Neolabatia parviflora is used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments. Its roots and leaves are said to possess anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. Neolabatia parviflora is also used to treat fever, wounds, and skin infections. Additionally, it is believed to have antiseptic and antifungal properties. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and safety for human use.

The plant's extracts have been tested in vitro for their ability to inhibit the growth of certain microorganisms and scavenge free radicals. Further research is necessary to fully understand the therapeutic potential of Neolabatia parviflora.

intro: Neolabatia parviflora is a plant species in the family Celastraceae, known for its medicinal properties. The health benefits of Neolabatia parviflora include its potential to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antipyretic activities, making it a valuable resource in traditional medicine. Bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Neolabatia parviflora are infusions, decoctions, and extracts, which are used to treat various health conditions, including fever, pain, and infections.
